---
title: "Federally Funded Mitigation Projects and Insurance Claims"
author: "Alea Wilbur-Mujtaba"
output:
  html_document:
    code_folding: hide
    toc: yes
    df_print: paged

---

Goal: Have historic mitigation projects and property acquisitions for selected states during the selected time frame. Once data is wrangled, an evaluation of the federal mitigation programs effectiveness will be provided. 
 
This analysis will also provide insights on buyout trends and larger mitigation efforts supported by federal programs.

# Data Wrangling

```{r setup, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::opts_chunk$set(message=FALSE, warning = FALSE, error = TRUE, tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

suppressMessages({
library(tidyverse)
library(mapproj)
library(scales)
library(usmap)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(tidycensus)
library(sf)
library(readxl)
library(choroplethr)
library(choroplethrMaps)
library(kableExtra)
library(gganimate)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)
library(plm)
library(pscl)
library(tigris)
})
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size=16))

CapStr <- function(y) {
  c <- strsplit(y, " ")[[1]]
  paste(toupper(substring(c, 1,1)), substring(c, 2),
      sep="", collapse=" ")
}

capitalize_str <- function(charcter_string){
  sapply(charcter_string, CapStr)
}

```


```{r}
# Population numbers until ACS data works better
us_states <- map_data("state")
us_counties <- map_data("county")


states <- c("Texas", "Florida", "Mississippi", "Louisiana", "Alabama")
population <- c(29730311, 21944577, 29161536, 4616106, 494969)
population <- data.frame(states, population)
```


## Mitigation Projects

Data downloadable from [FEMA website](https://www.fema.gov/about/openfema/data-sets).

NFIP CRS Community Projects & Mitigation Projects Data files:  

- MitigationProjects.csv: Historic mitigation projects contain information on federal grants distributed at county andcity level.  
- FEMA says these can be joined by projectIdentifier (beware of duplicates)  
- The mitigation projects file has each projectIdentifier and the number of properties that were mitigated.   
- Only kept 5 states in this practice data cleaning. Can easily edit `filter(stateNumberCode)` code to change state selection  


Hazard Mitigation Assistance Projects  

- The dataset contains funded **projects** (financial obligation to grantee) under the Hazard Mitigation Assistance (HMA) grant programs. FEMA administers three programs that provide funding for eligible mitigation planning and projects that reduces disaster losses and protect life and property from future disaster damages. The three programs are the Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP), Flood Mitigation Assistance (FMA) grant program, and Pre-Disaster Mitigation (PDM) grant program. This dataset also contains data from the HMA grant programs that were eliminated by the Biggert Water Flood Insurance Reform Act of 2012: Repetitive Flood Claims (RFC) grant program and Severe Repetitive Loss (SRL) grant program. 


Hazard Mitigation Assistance Mitigated Properties file    

- This dataset contains the **properties that were mitigated by projects ** funded under the Hazard Mitigation Assistance (HMA) grant programs. FEMA administers three programs that provide funding for eligible mitigation planning and projects to reduce disaster losses and protect life and property from future disaster damages. The three programs are the Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP), Flood Mitigation Assistance (FMA) grant program, and Pre-Disaster Mitigation (PDM) grant program. This dataset also contains data from the HMA grant programs that were eliminated by the Biggert Water Flood Insurance Reform Act of 2012 (BW-12): Repetitive Flood Claims (RFC) grant program and Severe Repetitive Loss (SRL) grant program   
- If these are collapsed by Project, they should have the values should be equal for amount spent?  
- The dataset contains properties by project identifier, city, zip code, state and region and does not contain any Personally Identifiable Information (PII). The mitigated property dataset can be joined to the OpenFEMA Hazard Mitigation Assistance Funded Project dataset by the Project Identifier field. Note, not all projects in the Hazard Mitigation Assistance Funded Project dataset will have mitigated properties (e.g., Planning and Management Cost projects). In some cases data was not provided by the subgrantee (sub-recipient), grantee (recipient) and/or entered into the FEMA mitigation grant systems. The information is likely available as part of the paper file which is considered the file of record.



```{r Projects}
# original FEMA file name was 
# HazardMitigationAssistanceProjects.csv

projectsmit <- read_csv("HazardMitigationAssistanceProjects.csv", 
                        col_types = cols(dateApproved = col_datetime(""), 
                                         dateClosed = col_datetime(format = ""), 
                                         dateInitiallyApproved = col_datetime(format = "") 
                        ))  %>% 
  filter(stateNumberCode %in% c(1, 12, 22, 28, 48),
         # 7,563 observations when including only the 5 states
         county != "Statewide"
         # 6,757 when "Statewide" projects are removed. 

         )
#Each projectIdentifier is unique in this document.

projectsmit %>%
 # unique(projectIdentifier) %>%
  group_by(programArea) %>%
  summarize(Count = n())
```

Each projectIdentifier is unique in object projectsmit. 

**Padding FIPS codes**

Writing a CSV file will drop zero placeholders in the beginning of numbers unless they are stored as a character (ex. 05042 would become 5042 but we need that first zero). 

Once state and county have 0 placeholders, then combine columns to make the 5 digit FIP code that exists in other FEMA documents.

```{r project-padding}
projectsmit <- projectsmit %>%
  mutate(stateNumberCode = str_pad(as.character(stateNumberCode), # StateNumberCode has to have 2 digits and adds a 0 placeholder if it doesn't
          width = 2, side = "left",  pad = "0"),
         countyCode =  str_pad(as.character(countyCode),  # countyCode has to have 3 digits and adds a 0 placeholder if it doesn't
          width = 3,  side = "left", pad = "0"), 
         countyFIP = paste(stateNumberCode, countyCode, sep = ''))  # combines state and county FIP codes for a 5 digit FIP county code
```

**Mitigated Projects Summary Tables**

Has number of projects and mitigated properties for every county, in each state, for every fiscal year.

```{r projects-summary}
projectssummary <- projectsmit %>% 
  filter(programFy > 1989) %>%
  mutate(state = tolower(state),
         county = tolower(county)) %>%
  group_by(state, county, programFy, countyFIP) %>%
  summarize(projects = n(),
            properties = sum(numberOfFinalProperties))

projectssummary
# 2,005 observations: projects and properties for each county during each year
# variables are state, county, programFy, countyFIP, projects and properties
```


```{r projects-per-state, include=FALSE}
##**Projects per state**  

#This shows the number of projects that occurred within each state:

projectsstate <- projectsmit %>% 
  mutate(state=tolower(state)) %>%
  group_by(state) %>%
  summarize(projects= n())

rownames(projectsstate) <- c("AL", "FL", "LA"," MS", "TX")
projectsstate  %>% kbl(caption = "Total Mitigation Projects in Each State, 2000-2019", 
                     ) %>%
  kable_classic(full_width = F, position = "left", html_font = "Cambria") 
```

**Properties per state from mitigated projects file**  

This shows the number of projects and properties (some projects contain multiple properties) that occurred within each state according to the Mitigated Properties dataset: 

```{r properties-in-projects}
properties_per_project_state <- projectsmit %>% 
  mutate(state=tolower(state)) %>%
  group_by(state) %>%
  summarize(projects= n(),
            properties = sum(numberOfFinalProperties ))

rownames(properties_per_project_state) <- c("AL", "FL", "LA"," MS", "TX")
properties_per_project_state  %>% kbl(caption = "Total Mitigated Properties in Each State, 2000-2019", 
                     ) %>%
  kable_classic(full_width = F, position = "left", html_font = "Cambria") 
```


## Mitigated Properties

- Mitigated properties data contains acquisition cost, date, eligible grant type in county, city, and property level.  

```{r properties}
#When data is redownloaded, original file called   HazardMitigationAssistanceMitigatedProperties.csv  

#MitigatedProperties.csv was already filtered to the 5 states in the sample frame. Add a filter() command and select for states 

propertymit <- read_csv("MitigatedProperties.csv") %>%
  arrange(projectIdentifier) %>% 
  filter(programFy > 1989) %>%  # has two logicals, get rid of them
  mutate(title = as.character(title), # no data in column
         numberOfRecords = as.numeric(numberOfRecords), #column doesn't have anything anyways
         actualAmountPaid = replace_na(actualAmountPaid, 0),
         stateNumberCode = str_pad(as.character(stateNumberCode), 
          width = 2, side = "left", pad = "0"),
         countyCode =  str_pad(as.character(countyCode), 
          width = 3, side = "left",  pad = "0"), 
         countyFIP = paste(stateNumberCode, countyCode, sep = ''))
propertymit # 32,932 observations

```

#### Buyouts

["Assessing the Full Cost of Buyouts" Paper](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10584-021-03178-x) by Curran-Groome et al. 2021
[Dataverse data and code](https://dataverse.unc.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.15139/S3/IARBJE)

"Efforts to assess and improve floodplain buyouts require an understanding of their full costs across the different levels of government involved, as is true of other devolved funding structures (Feiock 2001). While both federal agencies (e.g., FEMA 2013; FEMA 2021a) and academic researchers (e.g., Tate et al. 2016; Nelson and Camp 2020) have undertaken benefit-cost studies, these studies often conflate property purchase costs with total project costs, omit certain cost categories, or overlook the distribution of costs among actors. First, non-purchase costs associated with buyouts may be large due to the information-sharing, planning, and negotiation involved (Meyer et al. 2012). Second, non-purchase activities (e.g., communication, planning) are critical for enabling community-driven buyout programs, but it is unclear how much human and financial capital is dedicated to those activities across different buyout projects (Siders 2018). We aim to assess the full costs associated with buyout projects, in particular those costs imposed by federal policy requirements."

Other code examples -  github by [Kate Nelson](https://github.com/katesnelson/FEMA_buyouts)

```{r Relocation}

```

```{r Mean and Median Properties per Project}
buyouts <- propertymit %>%
  #tolower() %>%
    filter(
      propertyAction == "Acquisition/Demolition", ## filtering to floodplain buyouts
      str_detect(type, "200.1|200.2|200.3|200.4")
    ) %>%
    group_by(projectIdentifier) %>%
    summarise(n_properties = sum(numberOfProperties, na.rm = T))

# The number of properties acquired in the mean and median buyout project were:
  # Mean: 11.7
  mean(buyouts$n_properties, na.rm = T)
  # Median: 2
  median(buyouts$n_properties, na.rm = T)
```

The number of properties acquired in the mean and median buyout project were  Mean: 11.7 and Median: 2


```{r Joined Properties-Projects Dataset, include=FALSE}
project_ids_with_non_acquisitions <- propertymit %>% ##projects where there are mixed property_action values among different observations in the properties dataset
  filter(propertyAction != "Acquisition/Demolition") %>%
  distinct(projectIdentifier) %>%
  pull

project_missing_amounts_paid <- ##projects where there are missing actual_amount_paid values in the properties dataset
  propertymit %>%
  filter(is.na(actualAmountPaid)) %>% 
  distinct(projectIdentifier) %>%
  pull()

exclude_ids <- c(project_ids_with_non_acquisitions, project_missing_amounts_paid)

joint <- propertymit %>%
  left_join(
    projectsmit %>% #joining project-level data back to individual properties
      select(
        project_number_of_properties = numberOfFinalProperties,
        projectIdentifier, 
        projectAmount,
        projectType,
        projectTitle, 
        projectStatus = status,
        project_cost_share_percentage = costSharePercentage,
        projectAmount, 
        project_federal_share_obligated = federalShareObligated
      ),
    by = "projectIdentifier"
  )  %>% 
  select(-c(
    region, stateNumberCode, countyCode, disasterNumber, programFy, dateInitiallyApproved, dateApproved, dateClosed, title, numberOfRecords, granteeTribalIndicator, subgranteeTribalIndicator, id, projectType
  )) %>% 
  filter(
    propertyAction == "Acquisition/Demolition", ## filtering to floodplain buyouts
    type == "200.1: Acquisition of Private Real Property (Structures and Land) - Riverine" |
    type == "200.2: Acquisition of Private Real Property (Structures and Land) - Coastal" |
    type == "200.3: Acquisition of Public Real Property (Structures and Land) - Riverine" |
    type == "200.4: Acquisition of Public Real Property (Structures and Land) - Coastal",
    !(projectIdentifier %in% exclude_ids) #eliminating projects that contain a mix of property_action values or where there are missing actual_amount_paid values
  ) 
##nrow : 2675

grouped <- joint %>%
  group_by(projectIdentifier) %>% ## aggregating from property to project level 
  summarise(
    n_project_final_properties = first(project_number_of_properties),
    n_properties_summed = sum(numberOfProperties, na.rm = T),
    project_amount = first(projectAmount),
    acquisition_cost = sum(actualAmountPaid),
    transaction_cost = projectAmount - acquisition_cost,
    perc_acquisition_cost = acquisition_cost / projectAmount,
    perc_transaction_cost = transaction_cost / projectAmount,
    project_status = first(projectStatus),
    project_federal_share_obligated = first(project_federal_share_obligated),
    project_cost_share_percentage = first(project_cost_share_percentage),
    state = first(state),
    county = first(county),
    city = first(city),
    zip = first(zip)
  ) %>% 
  filter(
    project_status == "Closed", ## only closed projects, so all costs should be reported
    n_project_final_properties == n_properties_summed ## only projects where the reported number of properties is the same as the number of properties derived from the property-level dataset
  ) 
# 1479 
```

```{r FEMA Administrative Records - In-text Statistics, include=FALSE}
# There are 1479 floodplain buyout projects included in these data
grouped %>% nrow

# These 1479 floodplain buyout projects have acquired 5,586 properties

# The median, mean, standard deviation, and inter-quartile range of 
# percentage of project costs spent on "Actual Amount Paid" activities were:
  # Mean: 219%
  mean(grouped$perc_acquisition_cost, na.rm = T)
  # Median: 75%
  median(grouped$perc_acquisition_cost, na.rm = T)
  # SD: 288%
  sd(grouped$perc_acquisition_cost, na.rm = T)
  # IQR: 51% - 95%
  quantile(grouped$perc_acquisition_cost, c(.25, .75))
  
# Approximately 34% of projects spent two-thirds or less of their funds on 
# "Actual Amount Paid" activities
## % of projects that spend one third or more of funds on transaction costs  
(grouped %>% filter(perc_acquisition_cost <= .67) %>% nrow) / (grouped %>% nrow) 

# Many projects had "Actual Amount Paid" values that exceeded their total project costs
grouped %>%
  filter(acquisition_cost > project_amount) %>%
  nrow
```


```{r FEMA Administrative Records - Figure 3, fig.width = 7, fig.height = 5, eval = FALSE}
  grouped %>%
    mutate(perc_acquisition_cost = perc_acquisition_cost * 100) %>%
    arrange(desc(perc_acquisition_cost)) %>%
    select(perc_acquisition_cost) %>%
    mutate(intervals = cut(perc_acquisition_cost, right = T, breaks = c(seq(0,100,10), 200, 1001, 125160))) %>%
    group_by(intervals) %>%
    summarise(count = n()) %>% #%>% 
    mutate(
      order = row_number(intervals),
      intervals = as.character(intervals),
      intervals = intervals %>% as.character %>% str_replace_all("\\(|\\)|\\]|\\[", "") %>% str_replace_all(",", "-"),
      intervals = 
        case_when(
          str_detect(intervals, "1.25") ~ "> 1000", 
          str_detect(intervals, "1e") ~ "200-1000",
          str_detect(intervals, "\\[100") ~ "100-200",
          T ~ intervals
        )
    ) %>%
    ggplot() +
    geom_bar(aes(reorder(intervals,order), count), stat = "identity", width = .8, fill = "#767171") +
    geom_bracket(
      xmin = 11, 
      xmax = 13, 
      y.position = 83,
      size = 1,
      label.size = 3.2,
      label = "'Actual Amount Paid' costs exceeded total project costs." %>% str_wrap(width = 20),
      color = "#595959",
      fontface = "bold",
      vjust = -.5
    ) +
    theme(
      panel.background = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      axis.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 8, color = "#595959"),
      axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = "#595959")
    ) +
    labs(
      x = '\nPercent "Actual Amount Paid" Costs',
      y = "Frequency\n"
    )
```
```{r Sample Buyouts}
sample_df_buyouts <- propertymit %>%
    filter(
      propertyAction == "Acquisition/Demolition", ## filtering to floodplain buyouts
      str_detect(type, "200.1|200.2|200.3|200.4")
    ) %>%
    group_by(projectIdentifier) %>%
    summarise(n_properties = sum(numberOfProperties, na.rm = T))

# The number of properties acquired in the mean and median buyout project were:
  # Mean: 11
  mean(sample_df_buyouts$n_properties, na.rm = T)
  # Median: 3
  median(sample_df_buyouts$n_properties, na.rm = T)
```

**Joined Properties and Projects Data:**


This summarizes the number of properties mitigated within each county per year. This should be the same or similar to the mitigated properties summary above from the mitigated projects dataset (projects_counties has a properties variable). 


```{r}
propertysummary <- propertymit %>%
  group_by(state, county) %>%
  mutate(state = tolower(state),
         county = tolower(county)) %>%
  summarize(projects = n(),
            sumproperties = sum(numberOfProperties))

propertysummary # 382 rows. Total properties mitigated in each county ever
```



Filter for change in land use using the propertyAction variable. In the sample frame, ~324 properties were identified as some sort of Acquisition:
```{r}
#table(propertymit$propertyAction)

propertymit %>% 
  filter(propertyAction == c("Acquisition/Demolition", "Acquisition/Relocation", "Acquisition of Vacant Land")) %>% 
  group_by(county, countyFIP) %>% 
  summarise(properties = sum(numberOfProperties))

```



> Create tables for Projects, Project Applicants, and Allocated Grants. 
> data relations based on unique grant ids, applicant ids, and project ids

> Ideal situation: create interface that allows visualization by program type, data source, and time. City and property level summary tables that contain info on applicant, cost, historical losses and avoided losses (need tax assessor parcel data for this..? or HAZUS-MH data)

> BCA

- BCA for buyouts needs cost of acquisition and total avoided losses. Each property has data acquired and final cost. Total losses... Ratio of total loss and acquisition cost gives benefit-cost value for the property. If greater than 1, acquisition is considered successful. Data could also be aggregated to the city level. 

```{r Methods - Budget Review Sampling, eval=FALSE}
sampling_frame <- sample_df %>% 
  filter(
    lubridate::year(date_approved) > 2014,
    property_action == "acquisition/demolition",
    str_detect(type, "200.1|200.2|200.3|200.4")
  ) %>% 
  group_by(place, state) %>%
  summarise(
    county = first(county),
    date_approved = first(date_approved),
    sum_place_properties = sum(number_of_properties, na.rm = T) 
  )

sample_less_than_equal_50 <- sampling_frame %>%
  filter(sum_place_properties <= 50) %>%
  as.tibble %>%
  sample_n(50) # 5%

sample_greater_than_50 <- sampling_frame %>%
  filter(sum_place_properties > 50)

prelim_sample <- rbind(
  sample_less_than_equal_50 %>% as.tibble,
  sample_greater_than_50 %>% as.tibble
)

sample_counties_over_100 <- sampling_frame %>%
  group_by(county, state) %>%
  summarise(
    date_approved = first(date_approved),
    sum_county_properties = sum(sum_place_properties)
  ) %>%
  filter(sum_county_properties > 100) %>%
  anti_join(prelim_sample, by = c("county", "state")) %>%
  mutate(
    place = NA_character_,
    sum_place_properties = NA_real_
  ) %>%
  select(-sum_county_properties)

sum_county_properties <- sampling_frame %>%
  group_by(county, state) %>%
  summarise(sum_county_properties = sum(sum_place_properties))

final_sample <- rbind(
    sample_less_than_equal_50 %>% as.tibble,
    sample_greater_than_50 %>% as.tibble,
    sample_counties_over_100 %>% as.tibble
  ) %>%
  left_join(sum_county_properties)
```



## ACS Data & Census Data

There are two main ways to combine data from ACS with other data sources.

First method involves combining data based on a geographic ientifier that is available in both datasets (FIPS codes) or protected identification keys (PIKs) if using individual data. 

Reminder, ACS data is best for characteristics of the population and Census data is best for knowing the numbers of people in a geographic area. Compare 1-year estimates with 1-year estimates and 5-year averages with 5-year data. 

- I need 5-year estimates since I'm intersted in places that have less than 20,000 people. Another option for presenting ACS data for less populated areas is to show single-year estimates for large counties in Texas and then combine the remaining counties into a state “residual” by subtracting the available single-year data from the state total. Could also use PUMAS since they meet 65,000 population threshold for single-year estimates are often used as a substitute for county-level data.  
- When comparing 5-year estimates, compare ACS data over time based on nonoverlapping estimates. (2007-2011 compared to 2012-2016 estimates). 5-year estimates rely on pooled data   
- Data users should also consider changes in geographic boundaries, population controls, and inflation when analyzing trends with ACS data   
 
Used [their code](https://rpubs.com/dimuthu999/rvclimateriskJan2020) as an example and modified it. 
```{r censusapi, eval=FALSE}
#census_api_key("75e21d9ae279da76848e163aa0d638ed9f6705e6", install = TRUE)

#acs_variables <- read.csv(file="C:/Users/dratnadiwakara2/Documents/OneDrive - Louisiana State University/Raw Data/ACS Data/variable_names.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE,header = FALSE)

acs_variables <- c(White = "B02001_002", Black = "B02001_003", Native = "B02001_004", Asian = "B02001_005", HIPI = "B02001_006", Hispanic = "B03001_007")

var_labels <- c("B00001_001","B02001_001","B02001_002","B01002_001","B25099_001","B25003_003","B25003_002","B06009_004","B25034_001","B25035_001","B25034_002","B25034_003","B25034_004","B25034_005","B25034_006","B25034_007","B25034_008","B25034_009","B25012_002","B25012_010","C27001A_006","C27001A_007")

# B25012_010 # renter occupied housing
# "C27001A_006","C27001A_007" # with and without health insurance coverage 
# B25034_005 structure built

acs <- read.table(file=paste("acs_all_states_2016_2020.csv",sep=""),sep="|",stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

acs$GEOID <- as.character(acs$GEOID)
acs$GEOID <- ifelse(nchar(acs$GEOID)==10,paste("0",acs$GEOID,sep=""),acs$GEOID) # FIPS padding
```

```{r Florida Median Values, eval=FALSE, warnings = FALSE, message = FALSE, results='hide'}
flcounties <- get_acs(geography = "county", 
                 variables = "B25077_001", 
                 state = "FL", 
                 geometry = TRUE) 
plot(flcounties["estimate"], main=paste("Sum of Median Home Values", sub=" Data from ACS")) + # plot command from sf() library
  theme_void()

```





```{r iowamaps, include=FALSE, eval=FALSE}

####  Iowa Graphs Recreated

#Done to compare my numbers with numbers in  _An Integrated Flood Risk Assessment and Mitigation Framework: A Case Study for Middle Cedar River Basin, Iowa, US_ by Enes Yildirim and Ibrahim Demir. 
#Numbers don't match but inspect this again later. - April 13 2022

iowaprojects <- read_csv("HazardMitigationAssistanceProjects.csv", 
                        col_types = cols(dateApproved = col_datetime(""), 
                                         dateClosed = col_datetime(format = ""), 
                                         dateInitiallyApproved = col_datetime(format = "")  ))  %>% 
  filter(stateNumberCode == 19,
         county != "Statewide" )
#856 observations

#Each projectIdentifier is unique in this document.
iowaprojects <- iowaprojects %>%
  mutate(stateNumberCode = str_pad(as.character(stateNumberCode), # StateNumberCode has to have 2 digits and adds a 0 placeholder if it doesn't
          width = 2, side = "left",  pad = "0"),
         countyCode =  str_pad(as.character(countyCode),  # countyCode has to have 3 digits and adds a 0 placeholder if it doesn't
          width = 3, side = "left", pad = "0"), 
         countyFIP = paste(stateNumberCode, countyCode, sep = ''))  # combines state and county FIP codes for a 5 digit FIP county code

iowaprojectssummary <- iowaprojects %>% 
  filter(programFy > 1989) %>%
  mutate(state = tolower(state),
         county = tolower(county)) %>%
  group_by(state, county, programFy, countyFIP) %>%
  summarize(projects = n(),
            proposedproperties = sum(numberOfProperties),
            finalproperties = sum(numberOfFinalProperties))

# iowaprojectssummary
# 485 observations, each county- year that had at least one project

iowa_county_projects_summary <- iowaprojects %>% 
  filter(programFy > 1989) %>%
  mutate(state = tolower(state),
         county = tolower(county)) %>%
  group_by(state, county, countyFIP) %>%
  summarize(projects = n(),
            proposedproperties = sum(numberOfProperties),
            properties = sum(numberOfFinalProperties))

# iowa_county_projects_summary
# 99 observations, 1 for each county in Iowa with total properties ever mitigated in each county

# Make a map
left_join(us_counties, iowa_county_projects_summary, by=c("region" = "state",  "subregion"="county")) %>%
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
 filter(region == "iowa") %>%
  # summarize(project_count = n()) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group,
             fill = projects)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "gray90", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "light blue", high = "dark blue") + 
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Mitigation Projects Funded from 1990-2020")

right_join(us_counties, iowa_county_projects_summary, by = c("region" = "state", "subregion"="county")) %>%
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat,
             group = group, 
             fill = properties)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "gray90", size = 0.1) +
  coord_map(projection = "albers", lat0 = 39, lat1 = 45) +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "light blue", high = "dark blue")+
    theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Total Mitigated Properties from Mitigation Projects")


iowapropertymit <- read_csv("HazardMitigationAssistanceMitigatedProperties.csv") %>%
  filter(stateNumberCode == 19) %>%
  arrange(projectIdentifier) %>% 
  filter(programFy > 1989) %>%  # has two logicals, get rid of them
  mutate(title = as.character(title), # no data in column
         numberOfRecords = as.numeric(numberOfRecords), #column doesn't have anything anyways
         actualAmountPaid = replace_na(actualAmountPaid, 0),
         stateNumberCode = str_pad(as.character(stateNumberCode), 
          width = 2, side = "left", pad = "0"),
         countyCode =  str_pad(as.character(countyCode), 
          width = 3,   side = "left",  pad = "0"), 
         countyFIP = paste(stateNumberCode, countyCode, sep = ''))

#iowapropertymit         # 1720 observations

iowapropertysummary <- iowapropertymit %>%
  group_by(state, county) %>%
  mutate(state = tolower(state),
         county = tolower(county)) %>%
  summarize(count = n(),
            sum_properties = sum(numberOfProperties))
#iowapropertysummary        #  Properties mitigated in each county 
# 66 X 3 

#iowa_county_projects_summary      # compare
# 99 X 5


# get mismatched number of properties into one table to look at the difference
iowafulljoin <- full_join(iowapropertysummary, iowa_county_projects_summary, by=c("state" = "state",  "county"="county"))  # did full join to keep counties that had 0 properties
# properties from mitigated properties now almost match summed up properties from projects file. 100 counties included, even ones missing data, which is a good thing

iowaacquisitionsummary <- iowapropertymit %>% 
  filter(propertyAction == c("Acquisition/Demolition", "Acquisition/Relocation", "Acquisition of Vacant Land")) %>% 
  group_by(state, county, countyFIP) %>%
  summarise(AcquiredProperties = sum(numberOfProperties)) %>%
    mutate(state = tolower(state),
         county = tolower(county))
# 41 counties from iowa remain, 


# Make a map for properties
full_join(us_counties, iowaacquisitionsummary,
          by=c("region" = "state",  "subregion"="county")) %>%
   filter(region == "iowa") %>%     #990 obs remaining at this stage
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  # summarize(project_count = n()) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, 
             group = group,
             fill = AcquiredProperties))  +
    geom_polygon(color = "gray90", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "light blue", high = "dark blue") + 
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Total Acquired Properties")

```


### Maps Practice
```{r}
right_join(us_states, projectsstate,  
           by=c("region"="state")) %>% # State level data
  group_by(region) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat,
             group = region, 
             fill = projects)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "gray90", size = 0.1) +
  coord_map(projection = "albers", lat0 = 39, lat1 = 45) +
  coord_map(projection = "albers", lat0 = 45, lat1 = 55) +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "light blue", high = "dark blue") + 
  theme(legend.position="right",
        axis.line=element_blank(),
        axis.text=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        panel.background=element_blank(),
        panel.border=element_blank(),
        panel.grid=element_blank()) +
  labs(title = "Total Mitigaton Projects in Sampled States", subtitle = "1990-2019")
```

County summary data with all state outlines. Projects per year visualized by dots. Not the best graph but playing around with it for now:
Annimation attempt:
```{r}
countyprojectsgeo <- right_join(us_counties, projectssummary, 
                                by=c("region"="state", "subregion" = "county")) 

static_plot <- countyprojectsgeo %>%
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = us_states, aes(x = long, y = lat, 
                                     group = region),
             color = "white",
             fill = "grey") +
  geom_point(data = countyprojectsgeo, aes(x = long, y = lat, 
                                           size = projects, 
                    color = factor(programFy), alpha = .7))+
 # scale_size(name = "Scale", range = c(.1,3))+
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Mitigaton Projects per Year per County in Sampled States", subtitle = "1990-2019") 
static_plot
```

```{r include=FALSE}
#County outlines for all states:

countyprojectsgeo %>%
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  #summarize(Projects = sum(projects)) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = us_counties, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
             color = "white", 
             fill = "grey92") +
  geom_point(data = countyprojectsgeo, 
             aes(x = long, y = lat, 
                 size = projects, 
                 color = factor(programFy), alpha = .5))+
  scale_size(name = "", range = c(.1,3)) +
  theme_void() +
    labs(title = "Mitigation Projects per County per Year", subtitle = "1990-2019, Year as Continuous Variable")
  
```

```{r include = FALSE}
south <- us_counties[us_counties$region == c("alabama", "louisiana", "florida", "texas", "mississippi"),] 

# south

ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = south, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
             color = "white", 
             fill = "grey92") +
  geom_point(data = countyprojectsgeo, aes(x = long, y = lat, size = projects, color = programFy, alpha = .5))+
  scale_size(name = "", range = c(.1,3)) +
  theme_void() +
    labs(title = "Total Mitigation Projects in Southern States", subtitle = "1990-2019")
```

```{r include=FALSE}
projects_counties <- projectsmit %>% 
  mutate(county=tolower(county),
         state = tolower(state)) %>%
  group_by(state, county) %>%
  summarize(projects= n(),
            properties = sum(numberOfFinalProperties))
 # 426 state-counties

right_join(us_counties, projects_counties, by = c("region" = "state", "subregion"="county")) %>% # do left joining for all counties to show up 
ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), 
                color = "white",
             fill = "grey", size = 0.1)+
  geom_point(data = countyprojectsgeo, aes(x = long, y = lat, size = projects, color = programFy))+
  scale_size(name = "", range = c(.1,3)) +
  theme_void()
``` 

**Mapping county data**

Total number of projects within each county:
```{r}
right_join(us_counties, projects_counties, by = c("region" = "state", "subregion"="county")) %>% # do left joining for all counties to show up 
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat,
             group = group, 
             fill = projects)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "gray90", size = 0.1) +
 coord_map(projection = "albers", lat0 = 39, lat1 = 45) +
 # coord_map(projection = "albers", lat0 = 45, lat1 = 55) +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "light blue", high = "dark blue") + 
  theme_void()  +
    labs(title = "Total Mitigation Projects in Southern States", subtitle = "1990-2019")
```


```{r}
left_join(us_counties, projects_counties, by=c("region" = "state",  "subregion"="county")) %>%
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group,
             fill = projects) ) +
  geom_polygon(color = "gray90", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_gradientn("", colors = brewer.pal(9, "YlOrRd")) + 
  theme(legend.position="right",
        axis.line=element_blank(),
        axis.text=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        panel.background=element_blank(),
        panel.border=element_blank(),
        panel.grid=element_blank())  +
    labs(title = "Total Mitigation Projects in Southern States", subtitle = "With All counties and Different Colors")
```

```{r}
 filter(region == "florida") %>%
  # summarize(project_count = n()) %>%

             fill = projects)) +
```

```{r}
right_join(us_counties, projects_counties, by = c("region" = "state", "subregion"="county")) %>%
  group_by(region, subregion) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat,
             group = group, 
             fill = properties)) +
```

## Disaster Declarations

Data downloaded from FEMA's [OpenFEMA datasets](https://www.fema.gov/about/openfema/data-sets)  

- Create dummy variables for declarations in 2018, 2019, or 2020 instead of a factor but I could change this.    
  - Other option: Create factor for 1, 2, or 3 years since a disaster declaration. Proxy for recent event that increases the perception of risk  
  
[Significant Flood Events](https://nfipservices.floodsmart.gov/reports-flood-insurance-data)
  
```{r}
disasters <- read_csv("DisasterDeclarationsSummaries.csv") 
# 62977 X 23 as of April 13 2022

disasters <- disasters %>%
  filter(fyDeclared > 1989) %>%
      filter(state == c("AL", "FL", "LA", "MS", "TX"), # 2376 observations remaining for these states before other filters
             paProgramDeclared ==1,
             hmProgramDeclared ==1)  %>%  # 991 obs left
  mutate(stateNumberCode = str_pad(as.character(fipsStateCode), 
          width = 2, 
          side = "left", 
          pad = "0"),
         countyCode =  str_pad(as.character(fipsCountyCode),  
          width = 3, 
          side = "left", 
          pad = "0"), 
         countyFIP = paste(fipsStateCode, fipsCountyCode, sep = ''))  # combines state and county FIP codes for a 5 digit FIP county code

# potential way to do dummy variables for recent disaster availability heuristic 
disasters <- disasters %>% 
  mutate(
    flooding = sapply(disasters$declarationTitle,function(x) as.numeric(regexpr("FLOODING",x)>0 |regexpr("FLOOD",x)>0 | regexpr("HURRICANE",x)>0 | regexpr("STORM",x)>0) ),
    year2018 = ifelse(fyDeclared == 2018, 1, 0),
    year2019 = ifelse(fyDeclared == 2019, 1, 0),
    year2020 = ifelse(fyDeclared == 2020, 1, 0)) %>% # 202 without flooding, 789 with flooding before next line
  filter(flooding ==1 | incidentType == "Flood") %>% # 789 obs remaining
  select(-c(hash, lastRefresh, id, ihProgramDeclared:hmProgramDeclared )) %>%
  mutate(countyArea = gsub('[[:punct:]]', '', designatedArea),
         countyArea =paste(countyArea, state, sep=", ")) # %>% to lower(countyArea)

declarations_recentyears <- disasters %>%
  group_by(state, countyFIP, countyArea ) %>%
  summarize(total2019 = sum(year2019),
            total2018 = sum(year2018),
            total2020 = sum(year2020))
declarations_recentyears
#397 observations --> 397 counties had a disaster declaration involving some type of flood during 2018, 2019, or 2020.

# table(disasters$year2020) 
disasters %>%
  group_by(state)%>%
  summarize(Declarations = n())
# Ji and Lee 2019 make similar summary table and erge it with HMGP recipients per state

disasters_sum <- disasters %>%
  group_by(state, countyFIP, countyArea )%>%
  summarize(Declarations = n())
```
781 counties had disasters in 2018, 762 in 2019, and 745 in 2020. 

> Numbers don't seem right, check later

397 counties had a disaster declaration involving some type of flood during 2018, 2019, or 2020.
```{r eval = FALSE}
col_list <- c("gray90","gray85","gray80","gray75","gray60","gray50","gray40","gray20","gray10")
gr <-
  left_join(us_counties, disasters_sum, by=c("region" = "state")) %>% #,"subregion"="county")) %>%
  group_by(region) %>%
  
   ggplot()+
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group,
             fill = Declarations)) +
  geom_polygon(data = counties[!substr(counties$id,1,2) %in% c("02","15","72"),],aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group,fill=disasters),color="gray20")+
  geom_polygon(data = us_state[!us_state$id %in% c("02","15","72"),],aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),color="black",fill=NA)+
  # scale_color_manual(values=c("bisque3","midnightblue"))+
  # scale_fill_manual(values=c("bisque3","midnightblue"))+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",panel.grid = element_blank(),axis.text = element_blank(),legend.title = element_blank())+
  xlab("")+
  ylab("")+
  scale_fill_gradient(low="gray",high="black",na.value = "white")
  # scale_color_manual(values = col_list,na.value="white") +
  # scale_fill_manual(values = col_list,na.value="white")
  # ggtitle("Fraction of homes with A and V policies")
gr
```

## CRS Hazard Mitigation Projects

### Mitigation projects by CRS communities

Each project identifier is not unique in this file. Not sure how to use this yet or if it is needed.
 
- 47,674 observations  
  - 45,715 observations when "Statewide" is removed  
- Variables: projectIdentifier, communityName, communityNumber  

- FEMA website specifically says the nfipcrs dataset can match with mitigation projects dataset using the projectIdentifier field  
  - Can be used to provide communityName or number to files by projectIdentifier AND community name? ProjectIdentifier has many duplicates from statewide projects

```{r}
mitigation_nfipcrs <- read_csv("MitigationProjectsByNfipCrsCommunities.csv") %>%   
  # 47674 observations projects within CRS communities (total observations; when project identifiers are NOT unique)
  arrange(projectIdentifier) %>% 
  filter(communityName != "Statewide") %>%  #45,715 remaining
  mutate(communityName = gsub("\\*", "", communityName), 
         state = case_when(
           gsub("MS", "", projectIdentifier),
         )
         ) 


```

**Don't join this file to anything. The project identifiers have many many duplicates. It does provide community name and community number if that can be matched to other documents.** 

>.... Merge this the other way. Merge projectmit TO mitigation_nfipcrs!!!


There are 21,846 unique project Identifiers for ALL CRS communities and 17,830 unique community numbers for ALL communities in the US.

```{r eval=FALSE}
# https://www.fema.gov/openfema-data-page/hazard-mitigation-assistance-projects-nfip-crs-communities-v1
 mitigation_nfipcrs <- read_csv("MitigationProjectsByNfipCrsCommunities.csv") 

# dummy for if mitigation project was in CRS
propertymit$CRS <- ifelse(mitigation_nfipcrs$projectIdentifier == propertymit$projectIdentifier, 1, 0)

mitigation_nfipcrs %>%         # 45,715 communities
  arrange(projectIdentifier) %>% 
  group_by(projectIdentifier, communityName) %>% 
  summarize(projects = n()) 
# 45,693 unique observations when grouped by community name and project id
# so most are not duplicates or problemataic

# 20,029 groups: unique project Identifiers-CRS community combos

#distinctID <- ##projects where there are missing actual_amount_paid values in the properties dataset
  mitigation_nfipcrs %>%
  arrange(projectIdentifier) %>%
  distinct(projectIdentifier) %>%
  pull()


mitigation_nfipcrs %>%
  arrange(projectIdentifier) %>% 
  group_by(communityNumber, communityName) %>% 
  summarize(projects = n()) %>% # count of projects IDs within each community for ALL CRS communities
  arrange(desc(projects))
# 23,344 observations

# 17,773 groups:  unique community numbers - community names

```
 


## CRS Info

- Original file name: nfip_community-status-information_20220331.xlsx
- File downloaded from [NFIP Services](https://nfipservices.floodsmart.gov/reports-flood-insurance-data). Can also get file from PART website. 

- Previously used CommunityStatusInformation.xlsx file. Merged sheet 1 and 2 from excel file and created a combined file called CRSinfo.csv from combined columns and community information

There are 29,109 observations listed in CRSinfo file. 

There are 3,378 communities within the five selected states. This includes those that do and do not participate in the CRS program.

**To do **  

- Add in CRS status to the regression too eventually. Make a dummy variable for each CRS level (1-5 together,6,7,8,9,10 separately due to literature saying the later levels result in more mitigation.)

Communities within each of the five states that do or do not participate in the CRS:

```{r CRS-datacreation, eval = FALSE}
# sheet2 <- read_excel("C:/users/aleaw/OneDrive/Desktop/PhD Fall 2021 - Spring 2022/Project Data/RAW data/nfip_community-status-information_20220331.xlsx", sheet = 2)
# sheet3 <- read_excel("C:/users/aleaw/OneDrive/Desktop/PhD Fall 2021 - Spring 2022/Project Data/RAW data/nfip_community-status-information_20220331.xlsx", sheet = 3)
# 
# crs_combo<- left_join(sheet2, sheet3, by = c("State", "Community Number", "Community Name"))
#write_csv(crs_combo, "crs_info_april142020.csv")
```


```{r CRS-participation}
# created from code above - read in two sheets from downloaded excel file, merged, saved, and then opened again in R
communities <- read_csv("crs_info_april142020.csv") %>%
  filter(State == c("ALABAMA", "FLORIDA", "LOUISIANA",  "MISSISSIPPI", "TEXAS"))
  # 677 communities remain


 # CRS Indicator is Y if the crs_eff_dt has a value, otherwise Null. So if there was a CRS status in effect, then yes, otherwise null
CRS_communities_per_state <- communities %>%
  arrange("Community Number")%>%
  group_by(State, `CRS Indicator`)%>%
  summarise(count = n()) %>% 
    pivot_wider(names_from = 'CRS Indicator', values_from = 'count') 
CRS_communities_per_state


# 0 = None, 1 = Emergency Program, 2 = Regular Program, 3 = Direct entry
crs_participation <- communities %>%
  group_by(State, `Program Type`)%>%
  summarise(count = n()) %>% 
    pivot_wider(names_from = 'Program Type', values_from = 'count') 
crs_participation

# All communities' status within each state. This includes CRS communities AND all others. Current Status is the community's status in the National Flood Insurance Program
communities %>%
    arrange("Community Number") %>% 
 filter(`Community Status` == c("PARTICIPATING", "NOT PARTICIPATING", "OTHER")) %>%
  group_by(State, `Community Status`)%>%
  summarise(count = n()) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = 'Community Status', values_from = count) 

# NFIP community Participation count per state
communities  %>%  
  arrange(`Community Number`) %>%
  group_by(State, `Community Status`) %>%
  summarise("Number of Communities" = n()) %>%
 mutate(State = stringr::str_to_title(State) ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = 'Community Status', values_from = 'Number of Communities')
```


```{r CRS-participation2, include=FALSE}
### Pivot Wider for NFIP paritcpation community count summary table: ### 
communities %>%
  mutate(Status = case_when(`Community Status` == "DEFUNCT" ~ "OTHER",
           `Community Status` == "NOT AN NFIP COMMUNITY" ~ "OTHER", 
           `Community Status` == "RESERVED" ~ "OTHER",
           `Community Status` == "SUSPENDED" ~ "OTHER",
           `Community Status` == "NOT SANCTIONED" ~ "OTHER",
           `Community Status` == "PARTICIPATING" ~ "PARTICIPATING",
           `Community Status` == "NOT PARTICIPATING" ~ "NOT PARTICIPATING",
            )) %>%
  filter(Status == c("PARTICIPATING", "NOT PARTICIPATING", "OTHER")) %>%
                       group_by(State, Status)%>%
                       summarise(count = n()) %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = "Status", values_from = "count") %>%
  select("PARTICIPATING", "NOT PARTICIPATING", "OTHER")
```
> Doesn't make sense?

Number of communities in CRS. Reminder, communities are different that counties. There are many FEMA communities within a county.  Then Padding Community Number and dividing it up into the state and county FIPs. Makes a dummy variable for if the Community Name has "County" in the name.

```{r}
# creates community number, state number, and county numbers for matching later
CRSinfo <- communities %>% 
  mutate(`Community Number` = str_pad(as.character(`Community Number`), width = 6, side = "left", pad = 0)) %>%
  separate(`Community Number`, c("StateNumber", "Ignore"), #ignore is just the extra number we don't need
           sep = 2, remove = FALSE) %>%
  separate(`Community Number`, c("FullCountyNumber", "CommunityNum"), 
           sep = 5, remove = FALSE)

# creates variable for if the community is actually a county, city, town, village, or other

CRSinfo <- CRSinfo %>% 
  mutate(geo_type = case_when(
    grepl("COUNTY", `Community Name`) ~ "County",
    grepl("CITY", `Community Name`) ~ "City",
    grepl("TOWN", `Community Name`) ~ "Town",
    grepl("VILLAGE", `Community Name`) ~ "Village",
    TRUE ~ "Other"))

table(CRSinfo$geo_type)
```

Check if city names and community names are similar.

Join with FEMAClaims using the Community number:
```{r eval = FALSE}
CRSinfo <- CRSinfo %>% 
  mutate(firmstatus = NA,
         firmstatus = case_when(
    'FIRM Status Code' = 1 ~ "NotMapped",
    'FIRM Status Code' = 2 ~ "Intial", 
    'FIRM Status Code' = 3 ~ "Revised",
    'FIRM Status Code' = 4 ~ "Rescinded",
    'FIRM Status Code' = 5 ~ "allCXnoFIRM",
    'FIRM Status Code' = 6 ~ "allAACXnoelevation",
    'FIRM Status Code' = 7 ~ "ZonesACX",
    'FIRM Status Code' = 8 ~ "ZoneD",
    TRUE ~ "Non-paticipating")
    )

table(CRSinfo$firmstatus)


#left_join(propertymit, CRSinfo, by = ( = "Community Name"))
```


Now we have the number of claims each year for every county within the 5 selected states:
```{r}

```


Impact on CRS discount [code example](https://rpubs.com/dimuthu999/rvclimateriskJan2020)

## Insurance Policies and Claims

**Helpful website with data:** [Flood Insurance Data and Analytics](https://nfipservices.floodsmart.gov/reports-flood-insurance-data)

[BW House Prices](https://rpubs.com/dimuthu999/bwhouseprice)  
_log(premium)=pre−FIRM×year+countyFE+state∗yearFE_

### Policies


Download [here](https://www.fema.gov/openfema-data-page/fima-nfip-redacted-policies-v1)

Download HUDEX Report, Policy and Loss Data by Community from NFIP services [website](https://nfipservices.floodsmart.gov/reports-flood-insurance-data)



### Claims

**Dependent Variables**  
Flood losses will be based on FEMA’s NFIP insurance damage claims dataset. All insurance claims, property type, location, CRS level, and history of repetitive losses is included in this dataset. This variable will be continuous where missing data implies $0 in flood claims or damages. All dollar amounts for all variables will be adjusted to 2020 dollars. Flood claims are recorded at the community and individual level but only community level data will be used in this study. Given that the flood claims and damage assessments come from archival data, there is little room for perception-based validity issues when it comes to the observations used in the analysis 

_- Similar analyses/authors:_
- ______


**Independent Variables:**

FEMA’s NFIP insurance damage claims dataset. All insurance claims, property type, location, CRS level, and history of repetitive losses is included in this dataset.


Download from OpenFEMA [here](https://www.fema.gov/openfema-data-page/fima-nfip-redacted-claims-v1)


```{r}
#install.packages("rfema", repos = "https://ropensci.r-universe.dev")
library(rfema)

data_sets <- fema_data_sets() 
#data_fields <- fema_data_fields("fimaNfipClaims")

data_fields <- fema_data_fields("fimaNfipPolicies")

params <- parameter_values(data_set = "fimaNfipPolicies", data_field =  "policyEffectiveDate")

my_filters<- list(propertyState = c("AL", "FL", "LA",  "MS", "TX"),
                  policyEffectiveDate = "> 1989")
```


```{r}
##open_fema(data_set = "fimaNfipPolicies")

open_fema(data_set = "fimaNfipPolicies", top_n = 100, filters = my_filters)
```


```{r}
open_fema(data_set = "fimaNfipPolicies", filters = my_filters)
```


```{r}
#open_fema(data_set = "fimaNfipClaims", select = c("state", "reportedZipCode", "countyCode", "crsClassCode"), filters = my_filters)
```


```{r eval=FALSE}
df <- open_fema(data_set = "fimaNfipClaims",
                 top_n = 100,
                 filters = list(countyCode = "= 01001",
                                yearOfLoss = ">= 2010",
                                yearOfLoss = "<= 2020"))
```

```{r eval = FALSE}

#policies <- read_csv("C:/Users/aleaw/OneDrive/Desktop/PhD Fall 2021 - Spring 2022/Project Data/RAW data/FimaNfipPolicies.csv", filter(propertyState = c("AL", "MS", "TX", "LA", "FL") )
)


#library("fst")
#FL_policies <- read_fst(paste0(path,"policy_dataFL.fst"),from=1, to= 1000,as.data.table = TRUE)

# policies[,countycode:=substr(policies$censustract,1,5)]
# policies[,contentscovered:=ifelse(policies$totalcontentsinsurancecoverage>0,1,0)]


deductible <- data.frame(deductibleamountinbuildingcoverage = c("0","1","2","3","4","5","9","A","B","C","D","E","F","G"), buildingdeductible
= c(500,1000,2000,3000,4000,5000,750,10000,15000,20000,25000,50000,1250,1500),stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
deductible <- data.table(deductible)

# policies <- merge(policies,deductible,by="deductibleamountinbuildingcoverage")
# 
# policies[,maxdeductible:=ifelse((policies$policyeffectivedate<"2015-04-01" & policies$buildingdeductible==5000)| (policies$policyeffectivedate>="2015-04-01" & policies$buildingdeductible==10000),1,0)]
# policies[,maxdeductible5000:=ifelse(policies$buildingdeductible>=5000,1,0)]
# 
# policies[,nbyear:=year(policies$originalnbdate)]
```

```{r}
claims <- read_csv("FEMAClaims.csv")

claims <- claims %>%
  mutate(amountPaidOnBuildingClaim = replace_na(amountPaidOnBuildingClaim, 0),
         amountPaidOnContentsClaim = replace_na(amountPaidOnContentsClaim, 0),
         amountPaidOnIncreasedCostOfComplianceClaim = replace_na(amountPaidOnIncreasedCostOfComplianceClaim, 0)) %>%
  mutate(amountPaid = (amountPaidOnBuildingClaim + amountPaidOnContentsClaim + amountPaidOnIncreasedCostOfComplianceClaim)) %>% 
 filter(yearOfLoss > 1989) # all were already after 1989
# 377092

claims$countyCode <- 
  str_pad(as.character(claims$countyCode), 
          width = 5, 
          side = "left", 
          pad = "0")

CRSsummary <- claims %>% 
  group_by(state, countyCode, yearOfLoss, communityRatingSystemDiscount) %>% 
  summarize(amountPaid = sum(amountPaid),
            amountPaid = dollar(amountPaid))
CRSsummary
```


```{r}
claimssummary <- claims %>% 
  group_by(countyCode, yearOfLoss) %>% 
  summarize(amountPaid = sum(amountPaid),
            amountPaid = round(amountPaid, 0))

claimssummary
```

```{r}

claims %>%
  group_by(state, yearOfLoss) %>%
  summarize(totalclaims = n()) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = yearOfLoss, y = totalclaims)) +
  geom_col(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  facet_wrap(~state)

claims %>%
  group_by(state, yearOfLoss) %>%
  summarize(DollarsPaid = round(sum(amountPaid))) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = yearOfLoss, y = DollarsPaid)) +
  geom_col(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  facet_wrap(~state)
```


## Grant Award Summaries for Mitigation Funds

__Grant types:__   

- Public Assistance (PA) is FEMA's largest grant program providing funds to assist communities responding to and recovering from major declared disasters or emergencies.    
-FEMA assists individuals and households through the coordination and delivery of Individual Assistance (IA) programs. IA includes a number of other programs, including Individuals and Households Program (IHP) and that in turn includes Housing Assistance (HA) and Other Needs Assistance (ONA).  
- Hazard Mitigation Grant Programs: FEMA administers three programs that provide funding for eligible mitigation _planning_ and projects that reduces disaster losses and protect life and property from future disaster damages. The three programs are the Hazard Mitigation Grant Program **(HMGP)**, Flood Mitigation Assistance **(FMA)** grant program, and Pre-Disaster Mitigation **(PDM)** grant program.  

**Original file name:**

Assistance_PrimeAwardSummaries_2021-04-27_H02M35S54_1   

- downloaded last year on April 27 2021  
- AwardSummaries.csv was prefiltered to the 5 sample states  
```{r eval=FALSE}
awardsummaries <- read_csv("AwardSummaries.csv")
# 458 observations X 88 variables
table(awardsummaries$cfda_title)
awardsummaries %>% filter(cfda_title == c("BRIC: BUILDING RESILIENT INFRASTRUCTURE AND COMMUNITIES", "DISASTER ASSISTANCE PROJECTS", "DISASTER GRANTS - PUBLIC ASSISTANCE (PRESIDENTIALLY DECLARED DISASTERS)", "FEDERAL DISASTER ASSISTANCE TO INDIVIDUALS AND HOUSEHOLDS IN PRESIDENTIAL DECLARED DISASTER AREAS",  "HAZARD MITIGATION GRANT", "INDIVIDUAL FAMILY AND GRANTS", "INTEROPERABLE EMERGENCY COMMUNICATIONS", "PRE-DISASTER MITIGATION", "PRESIDENTIAL DECLARED DISASTER ASSISTANCE TO INDIVIDUALS AND HOUSEHOLDS - OTHER NEEDS" )) %>%
  rename(DGPA = "DISASTER GRANTS - PUBLIC ASSISTANCE (PRESIDENTIALLY DECLARED DISASTERS)",
         FDAIH = "FEDERAL DISASTER ASSISTANCE TO INDIVIDUALS AND HOUSEHOLDS IN PRESIDENTIAL DECLARED DISASTER AREAS",
     #    BRIC = "BRIC: BUILDING RESILIENT INFRASTRUCTURE AND COMMUNITIES",
         FDAIH_Other = "PRESIDENTIAL DECLARED DISASTER ASSISTANCE TO INDIVIDUALS AND HOUSEHOLDS - OTHER NEEDS") %>%
  group_by(recipient_state_code , cfda_title) %>%
  summarize(awards = n())%>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = cfda_title, values_from = awards)
```


```{r}
df <- full_join(projectssummary, claimssummary, by = c("countyFIP" = "countyCode", "programFy" = "yearOfLoss")) %>%
  mutate(amountPaid = replace_na(amountPaid, 0))

```


Number of observations for each year.
```{r}
df %>% group_by(programFy) %>% summarise(observations = n()) 
```



# Analysis - Panel Data  

Before this step, ideally the ACS data on population, education, and other demographics variables for the regression would also be merged together. The panel data set should contain information on CRS status, NFIP claims, total mitigation spending per unit of observation,and number of properties mitigated.

Ideally, the type of mitigation project would also be used for long term vs short term projects (like acquistion projects vs reinforced or raised buildings).

## Balancing an unbalanced panel

```{r}
dfungrouped <- df %>%  ungroup()  %>% select(countyFIP, programFy, everything() )

dfungrouped

dfungrouped %>% is.pbalanced() # Nope, not balanced

balanced2000 <- make.pbalanced(dfungrouped, balance.type = "fill") %>% 
  filter(programFy > 2000) %>%
  mutate(amountPaid = replace_na(amountPaid, 0))  # 10,380 observations
         
balanced2000 %>% is.pbalanced() # TRUE, it is balanced now
balanced2000
```

- Balancing adds rows for counties that did not have any claims data during a year. Now years for all counties go from 2001 to 2019 and panel is balanced (same number of observations for each county)

```{r}
ggplot(data = balanced2000, aes(x = programFy, y = properties, color = state)) +
 geom_point() + 
 geom_line() +
 facet_wrap(~as.factor(state))+
 # geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = F, lty = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Year",  y = "Number of Properties Mitigated") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Based on how I have the data currently set up, it appears as if the state does not have a large effect on the amount of insurance claims.

> Need to adjust claims for population size

```{r Variable defintions, eval = FALSE}
dep_takeup = "policies_fraction~"
dep_content = "contentscovered~"
dep_deductible = "maxdeductible5000~"

controls_nonsfha = "log(median_hh_income)+log(median_age)+log(white_frac)+log(college_frac)+log(total_population)+log(no_of_homes)+log(owner_frac)+log(lr_cost)+log(median_house_age)+log(house_price)+disaster_1+disaster_2+disaster_3"#

controls_sfha = "log(median_hh_income)+log(median_age)+log(white_frac)+log(college_frac)+log(total_population)+log(no_of_homes)+log(owner_frac)+log(hr_cost)+log(median_house_age)+log(house_price)+disaster_1+disaster_2+disaster_3"#

controls_sfha_content = "log(median_hh_income)+log(median_age)+log(white_frac)+log(college_frac)+log(total_population)+log(no_of_homes)+log(owner_frac)+contentprem+log(median_house_age)+log(house_price)+disaster_1+disaster_2+disaster_3"#

controls_sfha_deductible = "log(median_hh_income)+log(median_age)+log(white_frac)+log(college_frac)+log(total_population)+log(no_of_homes)+log(owner_frac)+deductibleprem+log(median_house_age)+log(house_price)+disaster_1+disaster_2+disaster_3"#


fixed_effects = "|censustract+year"
cluster = "|censustract"

endo_variable_1 = "log(climatechangereal)"
endo_variable_1_cat ="factor(climatechangereal_cat)"
endo_variable_2 = "log(worriedaboutclimatechange)"
endo_variable_2_cat ="factor(worriedaboutclimatechange_cat)"
endo_variable_3 = "log(climatechangeimpactpersonal)"
endo_variable_3_cat ="factor(climatechangeimpactpersonal_cat)"


instrument = "rep_gain*post"


time_period <- 2013:2019
```


```{r}
statelm <- lm(amountPaid ~  state, data  = balanced2000)
summary(statelm) # Not great. r-squared = 0.005
```

If we pretend that the number of properties mitigated is the only variable that predicts the amount that is paid in insurance claims, then we would use the model below. The number of properties is statistically significant, but it looks like each additional mitigated property increases the insurance claims by ~$73,000. This is unlikely the true relationship because both variables are influenced by external factors. For example, areas that flood frequently have large amounts of insurance claims and also are attempting to mitigate more properties, both of which are influenced by the events that occur. 
```{r}
naivelm2 <- lm(amountPaid ~ properties, data = balanced2000)
summary(naivelm2)
```

The amount of national flood insurance program claims appears to increase with the number of mitigated properties but one does not cause the other. This is mostly just for practice visualizing variables. 

```{r}
ggplot(balanced2000, aes(x = properties, y = amountPaid) ) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = F) + 
  labs(title = "Mitigated Properties and Amount Paid in NFIP Claims",
       subtitle = "2000 - 2019",
       x = "Number of Mitigated Properties",
       y = "Insurance Claims (Dollars)", 
       caption = "FEMA open datasets")
```


If we have the number of properties mitigated within each state as independent variables, the R-squared increases but is still very low. This model does not account for projects and damage that occured over time.
```{r}
lm <- lm(amountPaid ~ properties + state, data = balanced2000)
summary(lm)
```

### Adding Dummies


If we add dummy variables for different years, that is one way to control for events that happened in different years. Here, 2005 is statistically significant, probably because of the large number of insurance claims that occurred from Hurricane Katrina.

I also did this for recent disaster decarations due to the availability heuristic where people are more concerned about a threat that they remember more easily. 
```{r}
balanced2000 <- balanced2000 %>%
  mutate(d2001 = ifelse(programFy == 2001, 1, 0),
         d2005 = ifelse(programFy == 2005, 1, 0),
         d2006 = ifelse(programFy ==2006, 1, 0),
         d2010 = ifelse(programFy==2010, 1, 0),
         d2016 = ifelse(programFy==2016, 1, 0),
         d2018 = ifelse(programFy == 2018, 1, 0))

#Let's now simply pool the data together and use standard OLS
lm <- lm(amountPaid ~ properties + state +  d2001 + d2005 + d2006 + d2010 + d2016 + d2018, data = balanced2000)
summary(lm)

```

## Panel Data Methods

- `pdata.frame()` comes from `plm()` package

### Fixed Effects

Example on FEMA buyouts [github code](https://github.com/katesnelson/FEMA_buyouts/blob/main/FEMA%20models.R)
```{r }
# set your unit and and time variables when working with panel data  a
plm.countyyear = pdata.frame(balanced2000, index = c("countyFIP", "programFy"))

plm.stateyear = pdata.frame(balanced2000, index = c("state","programFy"))

#fixed effects with year dummies
#fem1b <- plm(amountPaid ~ properties + projects + d2001 + d2005 + d2006 + d2010 + d2016 + d2018, data = plm.countyyear,  model = "within")
# could treat fyDeclared as a factor too. 

# These were duplicated. Fixed by not grouping by "state" when the claimssummary dataframe was created. Observations had conflicting state abbreviations. Don't know why... :  12033-2004 �22019-2017�, �48361-2005

#summary(fem1b)
```

```{r}
#First we will calculate the properties mitigated for each state (could do county too)
balanced2000 <- balanced2000 %>%
  group_by(state) %>%
  mutate(averageProperties = mean(properties)) %>%
  ungroup()
```

This accounts for the time variable already and compares counties against themselves over time. Downside is you cannot see coefficients. 
```{r}
#twoways approach; no year dummies ( accounts for them already)
fem1 <- plm(amountPaid ~ properties  + projects, data=plm.countyyear, model = "within", effect = "twoways")
summary(fem1)

```

```{r eval=FALSE}
### LSDV approach

fem1b3=lm(amountPaid ~  factor(state) + as.factor(programFy) + properties + projects,
             data = balanced2000)
summary(fem1b3)
```

```{r eval=FALSE}
### Between Estimate

model1 <- plm(amountPaid ~ properties+ projects, data = plm.countyyear, model = "between")
summary(model1)
```




```{r eval=FALSE}
### First Difference

mod4 = plm(amountPaid ~ properties, data=plm.countyyear, model="fd")
summary(mod4)

#now if we want cluster-robust standard errors in PLM we  can use their following
coeftest(mod4,vcov=vcovHC(mod4,cluster="group"))
```




## Flood Zones - Maps attempt
Copied and pasted from NFHLattempt.R file. Only had this. Deleted file while organizing computer but kept code here just in case. Code chunk set to not run.
```{r eval = FALSE}
# install.packages("devtools")
#remotes::install_github("mikejohnson51/NFHL")
#remotes::install_github("mikejohnson51/AOI")
#install.packages("AOI")
# load libraries

library(NFHL)
library(AOI)
library(tidyverse_)


# Define an AOI around UCSB
AOI <- aoi_get(list("UCSB", 10, 10))

# Look at layerID 28 description
nfhl_describe(28)$Description
#> [1] "Labels for flood zones. "

# Extract Flood Hazard Polygons and filter
floodhazard <- nfhl_get(AOI, 28) %>% filter(SFHA_TF == "T")
```

# Dependent Variable

## Property Damage

Total property damage from floods, hurricanes, and severe thunderstorms as defined by SHELDUS. A county that experiences both floods and server storms might result in multiple SHELDUS records and to address this, the study defines counties that had experienced property losses owing to two or three natural hazards in the same year and removed all property damage duplicates. The DV is also transformed to a logorithmic due to the heavily skewed data to the right of zero. Finally, HMGP-funded projects will not have an effect the same year they are approved so leading DVs are used to deal with the 18-24 month delay between funding and completion.   
- ideas from Ji & Lee's 2019 article 

## Independent Variables

The main independent variale is the interaction between two vinary variables: First, did a county receive a hazard mitigation grant as a sub-grantee (`HMGP` = 1 if yes), and second, did a county declare a presidential disaster declaration at the time of the grant awarded (`Declaration` = 1 if yes). The interaction term estimates the HMGP impact by centering on county governments that experienced severe damage due to a natural hazard and received money to carry out mitigation projects in the area. Negative coefficient implies the county was awarded HMGP funding after a severe disaster and experienced less property damage from future natural hazards compared to other counties. 

## Control Variables

HMGP funding per capita ()
Socioeconomic (related to potential loss or harm related to disasters).   
- Higher levels of poverty and racial marginalizationlack financial and technical resources for pre-disaster prepardness and tend to be more vulnerable. Also may lack transportation for evacuating (Godschalk et al., 1999)  
- Race: Percent of population that is non-white, below the povery line, or in the median income bracket.
- Housing: median house value at county level (or lower if it exists!), housing units at county level. _This is directly related to the amount of property damage that can occur._

- Business characteristics: Ji and Lee (2019) controlled for business characteristics, such as industry type, that impact speed of recovery. Capital intensive industries such as manufacturing can experience greater losses and be slower to resume normal functioning then labor-intensive industries. Labor intensive industries are also more likely to implement pre-disaster prepardness (van der Vegt et al., 2015).

> Make a table of Descriptive Statistics for the DV, IV and Control variables (with mean, std, min, max, etc.)

## Model

- Fixed Effects: Ji and Lee (2019) used pooled OLS with State and Year fixed effects. State fixed effects are used because states review HMGP grant applications and distrubt migitation grants (GAO 2015). Differences in state capacity could contribute to variance in community resiliency at the county or lower level. Also controls for differences in property damage across counties within a state over time by controlling for state-level, time invariant characteristics and net of time trends for the DV. The county-level control variables are for time-varying characteristics at the county level (Ji & Lee 2019. 

The state fixed effects models enabled a comparison of counties within the same state, while holding state-specific factors (that is: state governments’ capacities to manage and administer the HMGP process; economic, political, and institutional factors; and the influence of the latter on local disaster management) constant.

- Time lag: A two year lead is applied to the DV in the main model so the number of years included in the analysis is X -2 years. If a county was adversely affected by a major disaster triggered by a natural hazard at time _t_ and the mitigation project plan has been approved, it may receive the hazard mitigation grant from FEMA a year after the disaster, denoted as _t+1_. HMGP$/capita is also at _t+1_ to match time point of grant receipt. Completion of hazard mitigation projects need to occur within two years of receiving funds and is expressed as _t+3_. Property damage that occured 1 year after the grant was received is at _t+2_.  Control variables are measured at same time as DV to control for their impacts on property owing to the natural hazards. 

- Model for Ji and Lee: 
Damage = Intercept + HMGP(1) + Declaration(1) + HMGPXDeclaration
  - State and year dummy variables were included for state and year controls.
  
Robustness checks:   

- 3 year leading variable for damage
- county fixed effects models to check for observed and unobserved county-level characteristics
